preamble: The transformation of a voiceless aspirated bilabial plosive (pʰ) into a velar nasal (ŋ) is a complex and relatively rare sound change, typically occurring through a series of intermediate steps. This shift involves significant alterations in both the manner and place of articulation, as well as the loss of aspiration and the acquisition of nasality.
phoneticEffects: This shift involves a chain of phonetic effects: initial lenition of the plosive to a fricative, followed by debuccalization (reduction to a glottal fricative), and finally, nasalization and velarization of the glottal fricative

examples.note: In Nyole, a historical development from Proto-Bantu *p to a velar nasal (ŋ) has been documented. This shift is understood to have occurred through a series of intermediate stages, including lenition to a fricative, debuccalization to a glottal fricative (h), and subsequent nasalization and velarization of the glottal fricative.

sources: Schoenbrun, D. L. (1994). The velar nasal in Nyole (E.35). In R. K. Herbert (Ed.), Language and Society in Africa: The Theory and Practice of Sociolinguistics (pp. 171-184). Witwatersrand University Press.

Ohala, J. J. (1975). Phonetic explanations for nasal sound changes. In C. A. Ferguson, L. M. Hyman, & J. J. Ohala (Eds.), Nasálfest: Papers from a symposium on nasals and nasalization (pp. 289-310). Language Universals Project, Stanford University.
